tata.FY2023-24: AMBIGUOUS: FY2022-23 is reported collectively for Tata Steel and its Indian subsidiaries while FY2023-24 onwards is standalone

sections.about: This metric measures the share of direct sourcing from MSMEs or small producers and is aligned to BRSR Principle 8, which covers inclusive growth, equitable development and preferential procurement. It is an Essential Indicator because it reflects whether procurement spend supports smaller enterprises rather than only large raw-material suppliers. A higher value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers:
In FY2024-25, Jindal Stainless led at 11.04%, Tata Steel followed closely at 10%, JSW was at 5.58%, and SAIL fell sharply to 5.13%; the prior-year comparison shows SAIL was the leader in FY2023-24 at 29.28%, while Tata was second at 9% and Jindal was lowest at 5.38%. The sharp SAIL drop contrasts with its FY2024-25 supplier-development disclosures: 56 vendor development programmes for MSEs, 27 special programmes for SC/ST and women MSEs, and ₹2,586.52 crore MSE procurement representing 35.32% of eligible procurement, but the BRSR note clarifies that MSE figures are only for materials procured, so the standardized “directly sourced” denominator appears more restrictive.

Structurally, integrated BF-BOF steelmakers with captive mines and imported coking coal will naturally route large spend through captive/inter-company, large mining, logistics, bulk commodity and technology suppliers. This depresses MSME share even where vendor development is active. Jindal Stainless’ FY2024-25 improvement is consistent with a stainless-steel procurement model that has more distributed supplier categories around scrap, revert, ferro alloys, service vendors and downstream ecosystem support, while Tata’s 9% to 10% improvement suggests incremental progress without a step-change in sourcing architecture.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2024-25, Tata ranked 2nd at 10%, trailing Jindal Stainless by 1.04 percentage points, or 9.4% below the leader on a relative basis. Tata’s rank held at 2nd in FY2023 and FY2024 and remained 2nd in FY2025, but the FY2023-to-FY2024 trend is not like-for-like because FY2022-23 is reported collectively for Tata Steel and its Indian subsidiaries while FY2023-24 onwards is standalone. The FY2024-to-FY2025 movement is directionally positive: Tata improved from 9% to 10%, while SAIL collapsed from 29.28% to 5.13%, JSW declined from 6.9% to 5.58%, and Jindal accelerated from 5.38% to 11.04%.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Tata Steel FY2022-23 is reported collectively for Tata Steel and its Indian subsidiaries; FY2023-24 and FY2024-25 are standalone. SAIL’s FY2024-25 BRSR separately reports MSE procurement as 35.32% of eligible procurement, but the standardized metric used here is 5.13%, indicating denominator and scope differences.
